ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bread food product class; no specific 'white bread with butter' composite entry exists. Closest defensible parent class used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A000E covers bread and rolls as a category. No specific composite code for white bread with butter exists; parent category used as closest defensible anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 2 slices of white bread (about 60 g total) spread with butter, commonly consumed at breakfast or as a light snack.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with butter — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F05020119 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with provenance marked canonical and confidence 0.95. All per_100g values are preserved from the HPB source entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 75 for white bread is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables (Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The composite food (white bread with butter) uses the white bread GI as the primary driver; butter contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (75 × 40.62 × 0.60) / 100 = 18.3 for a 60 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (368 mg/100 g), negligible potassium (4 mg/100 g), and saturated fat from butter (~9.5 g/100 g) creating an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio and contributing to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid white bread with butter due to its high sodium content (368 mg/100 g), near-zero potassium (4 mg/100 g), and saturated fat load; switching to wholegrain bread with avocado or unsalted nut butter substantially improves the sodium-potassium ratio and reduces saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 75) and high carbohydrate content (40.6 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g), causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit white bread with butter because its high-GI refined flour base (GI 75) and low fibre content (2.1 g/100 g) cause rapid blood glucose spikes; replacing with wholegrain or high-fibre bread and a protein-rich spread will significantly reduce the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~9.5 g/100 g, well above the 5 g threshold), trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g from dairy), cholesterol (43.45 mg/100 g), and high sodium (368 mg/100 g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of white bread with butter due to its high saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g), trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g), and elevated sodium (368 mg/100 g), all of which contribute to LDL elevation and hypertensive risk; substituting with wholegrain bread and extra-virgin olive oil provides a cardioprotective alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter (~9.5 g/100 g) and trace dairy trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) are pro-inflammatory; refined carbohydrate further promotes systemic inflammation with no anti-inflammatory offset.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid white bread with butter as the high saturated fat from butter (~9.5 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) are strongly pro-inflammatory; replacing butter with extra-virgin olive oil on wholegrain bread provides anti-inflammatory MUFA and fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with butter is a simple, widely consumed food consisting of sliced refined white bread spread with butter. The white bread is made from refined wheat flour, water, yeast, salt, and typically sugar and fat, producing a soft, mild-flavoured loaf with a light crumb and thin crust. Butter, a dairy fat product, is spread on the bread surface, adding richness, a slightly salty flavour, and a creamy mouthfeel. Per 100 g, this food provides 339 kcal, 8.13 g protein, 16.61 g fat (of which approximately 9.5 g is saturated fat and trace trans fat ~0.1 g from dairy), 40.62 g carbohydrate, 2.1 g dietary fibre, 368 mg sodium, and 43.45 mg cholesterol. The glycemic index is high (GI 75), making it a rapid glucose-raising food. Clinically, white bread with butter is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, low fibre), hypertension (high sodium, negligible potassium), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat, trans fat, sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory saturated fat and refined carbohydrate). It is commonly eaten at breakfast or as a snack and is a staple in Western and Singaporean household diets. Healthier alternatives include wholegrain bread with avocado, nut butter, or extra-virgin olive oil.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id F05020119, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). GI value of 75 for white bread is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central butter composition data as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this composite entry.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions for this composite HPB entry (white bread with butter) are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database. USDA FoodData Central (NDB 18069 white bread, NDB 01001 butter) provides fatty acid data for individual components but not for this exact composite ratio; therefore -1 is used pending a component-level calculation. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite HPB entry is not reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this composite food. 4. saturated_fat_g (~9.5 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (~0.1 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central butter composition data applied to the known fat content (16.61 g/100 g total fat) of this composite; these are approximations and should be confirmed with a full fatty acid panel. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (75 × 40.62 × 0.60) / 100 = 18.3 for a standard 60 g serving (2 slices with butter). 6. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a globally generic Western staple. No mandatory local-language names apply under the multilingual rules of PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md. G6 is satisfied as no cuisine-specific language requirement is triggered.
